1: New oracle. Chapter 34 is the great shepherd passage of Ezekiel — the indictment of Israel's failed shepherds (kings, priests, and leaders) followed by YHWH's declaration that he himself will shepherd Israel, culminating in the promise of David my servant as the eschatological shepherd-prince.
2: hinneh anochi el-haro'im ('I am against the shepherds'): the prophetic confrontation formula hinneni elekha (I am against you) — used for Gog (38:3), Tyre (26:3), Egypt (29:3), and here for Israel's shepherds. The same formula for Israel's leaders as for pagan nations: they have failed so completely as to be indistinguishable, in YHWH's covenantal reckoning, from the enemies of Israel. Ra'u et-ha'eder ('they shepherd the flock'): the charge — shepherds who only fed themselves.
3: et-hacheleiv tocheilu ve'et-hatzemer tilbashu ('the fat you eat and the wool you wear'): the specific economic exploitation — taking the produce of the flock (fat/milk, wool) without caring for the animals. Ha'berirah tizbachu ('the sleek ones you slaughter'): the best of the flock consumed for the shepherds' own tables. Et-hatzon lo tiru'u ('the flock you do not shepherd'): the inversion of the shepherd's role — consuming rather than serving.
4: et-hachallot lo chizaqtem ve'et-hachola lo-ripe'item ('the weak you have not strengthened, the sick you have not healed'): the four-fold failure — not strengthening the weak, not healing the sick, not binding the broken, not returning the strayed. Et-hanidachat lo hashevotm ('the driven-away you have not brought back'): nidachat (the driven/banished) — those scattered by force, including the exiles themselves. Et-ha'avudat lo dirashem ('the lost you have not sought'): the seeking function of the shepherd — to actively pursue the lost, not merely care for those who remain.
5: vatifutzena mibeli ro'eh ('and they scattered because there was no shepherd'): the consequence of pastoral failure is directly the scattering of the flock. Vatihyena le'oclah lechol-chayat hassadeh ('they became food for every wild animal'): when the shepherd withdraws protection, predators move in. The sheep's vulnerability is not their fault but the shepherd's failure. The political meaning is transparent: Israel's exile is the direct result of corrupt leadership.
6: yittu tzoni bechol-heharim ('my flock wanders on all the mountains'): YHWH calls Israel tzoni (my flock) — possessive, covenantal. The wandering on mountains and hills echoes the bamot apostasy — the high places where false worship happened are also the hills where the scattered flock grazes without direction. Ve'ein dorresh ve'ein mevaqesh ('and there is no one who searches and no one who seeks'): the failed shepherds are indicted by their absence from the two defining activities of pastoral care — searching and seeking.
7: Direct address to the shepherds. The second person masculine plural shifts to the indicted leaders specifically. The formula lachen ro'im shim'u devar YHWH ('therefore, O shepherds, hear the word of YHWH') frames what follows as legal testimony — they are called to hear the divine verdict on their conduct.
8: chai-ani ('as I live'): the divine oath sealing the following statement with ultimate authority. Im-lo ya'an heyot tzoni levaz ('if it is not because my flock has become plunder'): the swearing formula of divine resolve. Vatihyena tzoni le'ochla lechol-chayat hassadeh mei'ayin ro'eh ('my flock became food for all the wild animals, without a shepherd'): the accusation against the shepherds is grounded in the documented evidence — YHWH has observed his flock's condition.
9: The repetition of the summons to 'hear the word of YHWH' signals the formal legal proceeding. The shephalim (shepherds/leaders) are being tried in the divine court. The pattern of accusation — you have not sought, you have not strengthened, you have not returned — is the evidence presented at the trial.
10: hinneni el-haro'im vedrashhti et-tzoni miyadam ('I am against the shepherds and I will require my flock from their hand'): darash (to seek, require, hold accountable) — the same verb used for blood-accountability (9:1; 33:6). YHWH 'requires' the flock from the shepherds' hands: they were entrusted with the flock; now the owner demands an accounting. Vehishbatti et-haro'im mir'ot tzon ('I will cause the shepherds to cease from shepherding the flock'): the Davidic/royal line of leadership is terminated — the shepherds' commission is revoked.
11: ki koh amar Adonai YHWH hinneni ani-udarashti et-tzoni uviqashtim ('for thus says the Lord YHWH: I myself will search for my flock and seek them out'): the dramatic pivot — the shift from 'I am against the shepherds' to 'I myself will shepherd.' The emphatic ani-udarashti ('I myself will seek') makes YHWH the direct replacement of the failed human shepherds. No intermediate human shepherd is mentioned here — YHWH takes the task personally. This is one of the most direct statements of divine pastoral action in the entire Hebrew Bible.
12: keiro'eh edro beyom heyoto veton-tzone nifzarot ('as a shepherd inspects his flock on a day when he is among his scattered sheep'): nifzarot (scattered/dispersed) — the diaspora condition. YHWH will seek out the scattered ones (ken avaqesh et-tzoni) from all the places they have been scattered — with the specific mention of yom anan va'arafel ('the cloudy and dark day'): the day of the exile, defined by divine hiddenness, is the specific context of YHWH's seeking activity.
13: vehiotzetim min-ha'amim ('I will bring them out from the peoples'): the gathering-from-exile as a second Exodus. Uqibbatzttim min-ha'aratsot ('and gather them from the lands'): a universal ingathering — not just from Babylon but from all nations. The list of terrains — mountains, valleys, watercourses, inhabited places — mirrors the comprehensive scattering described earlier: the seeking is as comprehensive as the dispersal was total.
14: bimra'eh tov ir'em ('in a good pasture I will shepherd them'): mera'eh tov (good pasture/feeding place) — the pastoral ideal of rest and abundance. Vehar marom Yisrael yihyeh ne'vehem ('and on the high mountains of Israel their fold will be'): ne'veh (dwelling place, fold) — the settled, secure community. The same mountains where the scattered flock wandered without a shepherd (v.6) become the home pasture. Sham tirbatznah ('there they will lie down'): rest in abundance as the sign of restored wellbeing.
15: ani er'eh tzoni ve'ani arbitzem ne'um Adonai YHWH ('I myself will shepherd my flock and I myself will make them lie down, declares the Lord YHWH'): the doubly emphatic ani... ani (I... I) — YHWH as the personal shepherd who does both the feeding and the settling. The resting of the flock (arbitzem) is the image of complete security — the posture of animals that have no fear of predators. Compare Psalm 23:2 and John 10:11-16 where the same vocabulary structures the good shepherd imagery.
16: et-ha'ovedet avaqesh ve'et-hanidachat ashiv ('the lost I will seek and the driven away I will bring back'): the fourfold restoration program — seeking the lost, returning the strayed, binding the broken (lashevurah echavesh), strengthening the sick (vachalah achazzeq). These are the precise pastoral activities the failed shepherds of v.4 did not perform. YHWH does what the human shepherds refused to do. Ve'et-hashmenah ve'et-hachazaqah ashmed ('but the fat and the strong I will destroy'): the judgment element within the restoration — the powerful exploiters within the flock are not preserved in the restoration.
17: uve'atten tzoni koh amar Adonai YHWH hinneni shofet ('and as for you, my flock, thus says the Lord YHWH: I am judging'): the judicial address shifts to the flock itself. The judgment is now between members of the community — not just between the shepherds and YHWH. Bein seh laseh laelim velatayishim ('between sheep and sheep, between rams and he-goats'): distinctions within the flock — the powerful members who exploited the weak. The shepherd who protects also judges within the flock.
18: hame'at miqem mira'eh tov tiruu ('is it too little for you that you graze on the good pasture'): the indictment against the strong within the flock — they consume the best pasture, then trample what remains; they drink the clear water, then muddy the rest with their feet. The image is of resource hoarding and deliberate degradation of communal goods. What the powerful don't use themselves, they destroy for others.
19: vetzon mira'ai mirmesteihem tir'ena ('and my flock eats what your feet have trampled'): the weak flock suffers from the strong's waste. The pastoral imagery captures the dynamics of social inequality: the poor eat what the rich leave after trampling, drink the muddy water after the powerful have drunk their fill. This is the flock-level equivalent of the shepherd-level exploitation described in vv. 3-4.
20: lachen koh amar Adonai YHWH eleichem ('therefore thus says the Lord YHWH to you'): the judicial verdict begins. Hinneni ani shafatti bein seh biriyah uvein seh razah ('I myself will judge between the fat sheep and the lean sheep'): YHWH takes the role of divine judge within the restored community — not merely restoring the flock from outside enemies but adjudicating internal inequities.
21: ya'an betzad uvechatel tidehu ('because with flank and shoulder you have pushed'): the physical aggression of the strong against the weak — pushing with flank and shoulder, butting with horns. Ad asher pitzartem et-kol-hachallot ('until you scattered all the sick ones'): the weak members of the community have been driven out by the aggressive behavior of the strong. The pastoral metaphor captures real economic and social dynamics within the exilic community.
22: vehoshati le'tzoni velo-tihyena od lavaz ('I will save my flock so they will no longer be plunder'): the divine rescue declared — the condition of being prey ends. Veshafatti bein seh laseh ('and I will judge between sheep and sheep'): the intra-community justice that guarantees the weak are protected from the strong within the restored flock. The good shepherd both protects from external predators and maintains internal justice.
23: vahaqimoti aleihem ro'eh echad ('and I will raise up over them a single shepherd'): ro'eh echad — one shepherd, singular. Ve'ro'ah ethen ('and he will shepherd them'): the subject unexplained — then named: avdi David ('my servant David'). The Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:12-13) here made eschatological — not David returned but a David-figure yet to come. Hu yir'em vehu yihyeh lahem lero'eh ('he will shepherd them and he will be their shepherd'): the doubled role — doing the shepherding and being the shepherd — emphasizes identity, not merely function.
24: va'ani YHWH ehyeh lahem le'Elohim ve'avdi David nasi betocham ('and I, YHWH, will be their God and my servant David will be a prince among them'): the covenant formula completed — YHWH le'Elohim (YHWH as God) and am (people, implied from context). The Davidic figure is named nasi (prince/leader) rather than melek (king) — a distinction Ezekiel maintains throughout (chs 44-46), perhaps to distinguish the eschatological Davidic ruler from the discredited kings of Israel's history. Ani YHWH dibarti ('I, YHWH, have spoken'): the inviolable divine word sealing the promise.
25: vekarati lahem brit shalom ('and I will make with them a covenant of peace'): brit shalom — the covenant of peace, appearing here and in 37:26 in Ezekiel (also Num 25:12; Isa 54:10; Mal 2:5). Shalom in the covenant context is not merely 'peace' but the complete well-being of the covenant relationship restored. Vehishbatti chayah ra'ah min-ha'aretz ('I will eliminate dangerous animals from the land'): the restoration of safety in the land — the predators that plagued the flock when the shepherd was absent are removed.
26: unatatti otam usevivot giv'ati berakhah ('and I will make them and the places around my hill a blessing'): berakhah (blessing) as the defining characteristic of the restored land. Vehora'deti haggeshem be'itto ('and I will send down rain in its season'): the seasonal rain as covenant blessing (Lev 26:4; Deut 11:14) restored. Gishme verakhah yihyu ('rains of blessing there will be'): the Hebrew plays on the sound — gashm (rain) connected to berakhah (blessing) — the material blessing is itself a form of divine speech.
27: veyitan etz hassadeh et-piryou ('and the tree of the field will give its fruit'): the restoration of agricultural fertility — the reversal of the covenant curses of Leviticus 26:20 ('your trees will give no fruit') and Deuteronomy 28:40-42. Veyad'u ki-ani YHWH beshivri et-motot ullam ('and they will know that I am YHWH when I break the bars of their yoke'): the broken yoke as liberation from Babylonian domination — the servitude symbolized by the yoke Ezekiel wore as a sign act (ch 4).
28: velo-yihyu od bevaz lagoyim ('and they will no longer be plunder for the nations'): the reversal of the condition described in v.5 and v.22. Uvat-ha'aretz lo tochlem od ('and the wild animals of the land will not devour them any more'): both human predators (nations) and environmental predators (wild animals) are eliminated from the picture of restoration. Veyashvu lavetach ve'ein macharid ('and they will dwell securely with none to make them afraid'): the lavetach (security) formula from Lev 26:5 — the covenant blessing of unafraid habitation restored.
29: vehaqimoti lahem matta leshm ('and I will raise up for them a planting for a name/renown'): matta (planting, cutting, shoot) — the agricultural image of divine planting. Leshm can mean 'for a name' or 'for renown' — the planted community that will be the glory of the restored land. Velo-yihyu od asufei ra'av ba'aretz ('and they will no longer be gathered by famine in the land'): the famine that drove scattered Israel is ended. The planted people (matta) deliberately echoes the agricultural restoration throughout — YHWH as the divine gardener.
30: veyad'u ki-ani YHWH Eloheihem ittam ('and they will know that I, YHWH their God, am with them'): the recognition formula takes its fullest covenantal form — not merely yeda'u ki-ani YHWH (they will know I am YHWH) but Eloheihem ittam (their God, with them). The divine presence is the content of the covenant formula. Vehem ami beit Yisrael ('and they are my people, the house of Israel'): the covenant formula completed — ani Eloheihem vehem ami (I am their God and they are my people). The full bilateral covenant restored.
31: ve'atten tzoni tzon mar'iti adam atem ('and you are my flock, the flock of my pasture — you are human beings'): the chapter closes with the affirmation that the restored Israel is both tzoni (my flock — covenant possession) and adam (human beings — dignity). The pastoral metaphor has a limit: the sheep are people. The divine-shepherd relationship does not reduce humanity to mere livestock but uses the pastoral image to convey intimate care and protection. Va'ani Eloheichem ne'um Adonai YHWH ('and I am your God, declares the Lord YHWH'): the covenant seal on the chapter.
